data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The simultaneous collapse was an emergent property of synchronized debt-deflation spirals in palace economies where redistributive storage and feasting obligations became actuarially insolvent when compound-interest agricultural loans to territorial subalterns exceeded carrying capacity","mechanism": "Palace economies functioned as predatory financiers extracting surplus through administered debt; cross-polity emulation of Mycenaean/Hittite loan-instruments created structurally homologous fragility; when harvest volatility increased (regardless of cause), debt renunciation cascaded through peer-guaranteed obligations, while Egypt's scribal debt-jubilee traditions (documented from Old Kingdom) provided institutional circuit-breakers","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "non-palatial tribal formations"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Extensive documentation of palace-centred credit/debt instruments in Ugarit or Hittite archives comparable to Mesopotamian practice", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Archaeological signatures of systematic palace burning correlating with debt-recording archives specifically", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian jubilee-year practice being demonstrably operative and not merely ideological in New Kingdom economic management", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Anachronistically imports Graeber-esque debt-critique into Bronze Age; assumes financialization without direct evidence for Hittite/Ugarit cases. The Egyptian jubilee comparison is especially speculative."}
